macro_segments.title: Purchasing eggs from non-Jews: The requirement for merchant's claim and testable specification

micro_segments.title: The basic permissibility of purchasing eggs from non-Jews without concern for neveilah or tereifah
Why non-kosher birds are a concern and the merchant's claim of a specific kosher species
The role of comparison and testability in validating the merchant's claim about a particular bird species
The principle of milsa d'avida legluya: reliability through public verifiability and risk of business reputation

macro_segments.title: Visual indicators of kosher eggs and their limitations as proof

micro_segments.title: The equation between indicators of kosher eggs and indicators of kosher fish eggs
The shape characteristics of kosher eggs: one rounded end and one pointed end
The white and yolk arrangement: albumen outside and yolk inside as a kosher indicator
Mixed or inverted egg contents as indicators of non-kosher species and rodent eggs
Why chopped, scrambled, and mixed eggs cannot be purchased from non-Jews: the ban on unverifiable eggs

macro_segments.title: Simanim lav de'oraita: indicators are insufficient for biblical reliance and require merchant's specific claim

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Zeira's principle that egg indicators are not biblically sufficient for determination
The eight uncertain birds and the failed proposal to use egg characteristics to resolve their status
Negative indicators as absolute proof of non-kashrut versus positive indicators as necessary but insufficient
Raven eggs resemble dove eggs: why visual similarity prevents reliance on indicators alone
Combination of correct indicators and merchant's testable claim required for reliance

macro_segments.title: The practical application by the Rishonim: Tosafot on purchases from non-Jews in medieval Europe

micro_segments.title: The majority assumption: eggs are from kosher birds where no alternative sources exist
Breads and pastries containing eggs: relying on the prevalence of kosher eggs and non-tereifah birds
Departure from Talmudic stringencies when practical concerns do not materialize in local context

macro_segments.title: Embryonic reptile eggs and the status of mixed or inverted contents

micro_segments.title: Why identifying an egg as reptilian matters: tumah status differs from non-kosher bird eggs
The problem of an embryo forming without a hole in the egg: whether tumah applies
Ravina's challenge: reptiles without tumah status and the need for specific reptile identification
Rava's answer: the transgression is against non-kosher creeping things, not tumah
Why an embryo matters: the distinction between prohibited derivatives and the animal itself
Whether unfertilized embryos of kosher birds are subject to the creeping-thing prohibition: rabbinic status only

macro_segments.title: Blood spots on eggs: location, source, and when the whole egg is forbidden

micro_segments.title: Unfertilized brooded eggs with blood spots: throwing out the spot and eating the rest
Rabbi Yirmeyah on the kesher: blood spots on the white versus on the yolk
Rabbi Dostai: only on the white is it a local problem; on the yolk the whole egg is forbidden
The why question: whether blood spots are biblical or rabbinic prohibitions
The reversal debate: whether Abaye reversed the original teaching or confirmed it

macro_segments.title: Deriving the prohibition on non-kosher bird eggs from the term bat ha-ya'anah (ostrich)

micro_segments.title: Chizkiyah's derivation: ostriches do not have daughters via live birth, so bat ha-ya'anah means the egg
The Gemara's consideration whether bat ha-ya'anah is simply the ostrich's name, not a reference to eggs
Scriptural parallels: uses of bat and bnot in other verses to clarify whether they mean offspring or the animal itself

topical_tags.term: milsa d'avida legluya (matter easily revealed/publicly verifiable)
simanim lav de'oraita (indicators not biblically sufficient)
masiach lefi tumo (speaking to his innocence, not for vested interest)
uman lo marei umnutei (expert does not ruin his craft/business)
raven egg vs dove egg (similar appearance)
avor min hachai (limb from a living animal)
giuul beitzim (eggs that have spewed out)
asmachta (support or hint, not primary source)
bat ha-ya'anah (ostrich, literally daughter of ostrich)
